summary: Added AI-Consumability dimension (5th and final). Created prompts/ai-consumability.md with verbatim rubric levels. Patched prepare.py to substitute {llms_txt} placeholder for multi-input dimensions. Scored all 10 pilot pages. avg=3.5, dist 3:5/4:5/5:0. All 10 pages are in llms.txt (✅). Zero pages have API-named headings — this blocks every page from score 5. The clean half/half 3:4 split reflects a clear structural pattern: pages where code blocks are preceded by headings score 4; pages where code follows inline prose sentences score 3.

3_lowest_scored_pages.rationale: 18 code blocks interleaved within flowing prose — worst extractability of the set. Description describes genre ('This comprehensive guide') rather than task.
11 interleaved blocks AND description opens with 'Comprehensive guide to...' — both anti-patterns present.
8 interleaved blocks across 3 operations — each operation's examples follow prose rather than a dedicated heading.

notes.llms_txt_multi_input_assessment: The multi-input approach (page + llms.txt) worked cleanly for Level 5 checking. All 10 pages appeared in llms.txt, so llms.txt presence was not a discriminating factor in v1. In v2, checking whether llms-full.txt accurately represents page content (not just the link) would add signal. The {llms_txt} injection via prepare.py is correct and generalizable.
notes.dominant_finding: Zero pages have API-named headings — this is a uniform blocker for score 5 across the entire pilot set. Adding headings like '## MessageRepository.flagMessage()' to 10 pages is a well-scoped, mechanical improvement task. The other blocker (interleaved snippets) affects 5 pages.
notes.prepare_py_change: Patched prepare.py line 139 to substitute {llms_txt} placeholder with contents of repo-root llms.txt when present. Backward-compatible — existing dimensions without {llms_txt} in template are unaffected.
